title: Hannah Dawson on Mary Wollstonecraft - Philosophy Bites
text:
Search

## Hannah Dawson on Mary Wollstonecraft

In this episode of the Philosophy Bites podcast Nigel Warburton interviews Hannah Dawson (editor of _The Penguin Book of Feminist Writing_ ) on Mary Wollstonecraft and her book _A Vindication of the Rights of Woman_ (1792).

* [Published 27 September, 2023](https://philosophybites.com/2023/09/27/)

00:00 / 00:00

### TRANSCRIPT

**Host 1:** This is Philosophy Bites with me, Nigel Warburton. And me, David Edmonds. If you enjoy Philosophy Bites, please support us. We’re currently unfunded, and all donations would be gratefully received. For details, go to [www.philosophybites.com](https://www.philosophybites.com)

**Host 2:** She’s one of the founding mothers of feminist philosophy. Mary Wollstonecraft was the author of a vindication of the rights of women. Hannah Dawson is an historian of ideas at King’s College London. Here she explains why Wollstonecraft believed that the fundamental right was the right to a particular kind of freedom.

**Host 1:** Hannah Dawson, welcome to Philosophy Bites.

**Guest:** Hello, Nigel. It’s lovely to be here.

**Host 1:** The topic we’re gonna focus on is Mary Boulston Kraft’s The Vindication of the Rights of Woman. But before we get on to the book, could you just say a little bit about Mary Boulston Kraft? Because she was quite a remarkable woman.

**Guest:** Yes. She was. She had the most extraordinary, adventurous, dramatic, tragic, short life. And I will tell you something of that life, but I also want to be careful, immediately kind of sound a note a note of caution in thinking about her life because so often she and indeed other women philosophers are reduced to the personal circumstances of their lives. You know?

**Guest:** We think of Simone de Beauvoir in relation to Sartre, and we think of Mary Wollstonecraft in relation to the tragedies of her life. And so I think it’s important in relation to women thinking and women philosophers that we give them the privilege that we afford to men, which is to untether them from their particular circumstances. However, it is true that she had the most extraordinary life. Yes. So she was born in the middle of the eighteenth century into a kind of classic patriarchal household with a feckless father who of course had all the power, who ruined the family financially, who favored her brother and educated him, did not educate his daughters, his brilliant daughter Mary, who forever regretted and resented that deprivation of education.

**Guest:** And it became a kind of crucial feature of her philosophy and indeed of early modern feminism more generally that education was a key to women’s liberation. And its denial was a totally significant way in which the patriarchy operated. So as soon as possible, she left home and she wanted to make her way independently in the world, earn her own money. And that notion of independence is also a crucial thread that will run through her philosophy as we will see. She went to be a ladies maid in Bath and then she had to come home to look after her mother who was sick, who then died.

**Guest:** She intervened in her sister’s unhappy, possibly abusive marriage, helping her to escape from her husband, after which her sister’s baby died. She set up a school with her sister and her best friend, Fanny Blood, in Stoke Newington in London to try to support themselves and support this community of women. That failed, and her friend Fanny Blood died along with her baby very soon after she had delivered that child. She went to France. She went to Scandinavia.

**Guest:** She was the lover of the American Gilbert Imle. They didn’t marry. She had a child by him. She named her child Fanny after her best friend. Gilbert behaved in such a unfaithful and problematic way towards her.

**Guest:** She tried to kill herself twice. She then took up with William Godwin, the anarchist, and she had a baby by him who was Mary, who would become Mary Shelley, the author of Frankenstein. And Mary Wollstonecraft died as a result of that childbirth from septicemia a few days after. And then as a kind of coda to this story, her first daughter, Fanny, killed herself later on. So you see, there is this extraordinary trajectory.

**Guest:** And as I say, I’m keen that we release her from that story and let her, alongside other male philosophers, as it were, stand on the kind of mountaintop of reason and give their thoughts on freedom and equality alongside Rousseau, you know, and Kant.

**Host 1:** Let’s turn to the book then, the book that we’re gonna talk about, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman. What was that about?

**Guest:** Well, before The Vindication of the Rights of Woman, she had written a a number of works. She’d written a novel. She’d written a book about the education of daughters. And she actually made her name, first off, not with The Vindication of the Rights of Woman, but in fact, an earlier text, seventeen ninety, The Vindication of the Rights of Men. That was in response to Burke, to Edmund Burke, who’d written his reflections upon the revolution in France, where he’d criticized the French Revolution as this terrible event whereby the revolutionaries had tried to remake the world on the basis of reason and liberty, and they burned the house down in the process.

**Guest:** And by contrast to entirely revolutionizing making the world anew, Burke thought that we should look to history, we should look to civilization as it was, as this demonstration of the best way in which reason evolutionarily, gradually unfolds in the world. What you have in history, what you have in the status quo is as it were the best scenario that we’ve worked out thus far. And therefore, we should look to it as a repository of kind of empirical wisdom rather than ripping it all up and starting again on the basis of abstract principles. And by contrast, what Wollstonecraft wanted to do was say, no. Let us tear up the old dispensation of hierarchy and arbitrary power, and exactly remake the world anew on the principles of freedom and equality.

**Guest:** And as part of that, she said, even in the vindication of the rights of men, what was crucial to that enterprise was thinking about what happened in the relations between men and women. It wasn’t enough as it were to revolutionize the public sphere. What you needed to do was attend to the effects of subjection to the arbitrary power of men. And only then would you have the total reformation of mankind that she saw as central to the political project. So when she went in to write The Vindication of the Rights of Woman, that was her ongoing intention.

**Guest:** She was interested in the reformation of society, in the moral reformation of society. And I think it’s important to note that kind of that moral core to her work. Of course, The Vindication of the Rights of Woman sounds like it’s a book about rights. And in a sense, it absolutely is. But she’s talking about rights in a very specific way there.

**Guest:** She’s looking to France. She’s thinking of France again. And she’s saying that if it’s the case, as the French revolutionaries had argued, that men have rights. So therefore, on their own terms, should women have rights insofar as women are also rational creatures. That’s to say they’re capable of self government through the exercise of reason over their passions.

**Guest:** And therefore, it is unjust. It is an illegitimate usurpation for anybody else to assume power over them without consent. That’s tyranny because these people are self governing individuals. Women are self governing individuals. And so there you see this very intimate link between the notion of a right and the capacity for reason.

**Guest:** One has the right to self government if one has the capacity to self government. That’s to say if one has the capacity to govern oneself with reason. And And that is why early modern feminists across the board needed to prove that women had reason. Because if women didn’t have reason, then they didn’t deserve the freedom for which they fought.

**Host 1:** It sounds very strange today that any intelligent person should doubt that women have reason. Why was that such a prevalent view?

**Guest:** Yes. Well, that’s a very deep question. I’m sure that somewhere at its base, the answer has something to do with power and vested interest and it being a very convenient piece of ideology to shore up men’s power. But there’s a kind of related bolt on reason which feminists were themselves very sensitive to, which was that women were deprived for the most part of education. That’s to say, they weren’t given the opportunity to develop their minds, to develop their reason in order to be able to deserve freedom.

**Guest:** And it’s a very striking and sometimes uncomfortable feature of early modern feminism that running through it, it’s not just a criticism of patriarchy, but it’s a criticism of women, of women’s corrupt minds, of women’s weak minds. Wilson Kraft is not saying women’s minds are naturally weak, but on account of the fact that they’ve been deprived of education, they haven’t been allowed, afforded the capacity to develop their minds and therefore they are in some sense de facto inferior to men. And therefore the subjection of them is understandable given the facts on the ground. I mean, doesn’t go as far as to say that, but it is absolutely the case that insofar as women were deprived of education, the thought was that their reason was insufficiently developed to warrant self government.

**Host 1:** Now you’ve talked about this book as being about freedom. What did Wollstonecraft mean by freedom?

**Guest:** Yes. Although the title of the book is The Vindication of the Rights of Woman, it seems to me that the fundamental right that she’s really interested in vindicating is the right to independence, to self government. And that is the right to freedom. I think it’s very helpful to think of her work through the lens not so much of rights discourse with all its associations with liberalism and individualism, but to think rather of her through the discursive lens of republicanism and what Quentin Skinner has called neo Romanism. And that’s a very particular kind of freedom that I think is totally core to Wollstonecraft’s work.

**Guest:** It’s the idea, as Quentin Skinner says, that English parliamentarians, for example, picked up from ancient Roman law, which is that the fundamental distinction between human beings is that between a free person, a libahomo, and a slave, a servus. And the free person is under their own jurisdiction, lives under their own will. The slave lives subject to the arbitrary power of another, of their master. And what’s crucial about that theory is that the slave need not be interfered with, need not be actually stopped from doing anything, need not be constrained or impeded in a kind of liberal sense. That’s say to all intents and purposes, a slave might seem completely free because the slave can go about their business as they want.

**Guest:** But if nonetheless, they do that at the mercy of their master, of another, if they’re subject to the arbitrary power of another, then they are unfree. And this idea appealed so deeply to early modern feminists who saw it as describing exactly the relation between husband and wife and more generally between men and women in society. Because it might be that you have a kind husband. It might be that your husband lets you do what you want. So you’re not unfree in the kind of liberal sense.

**Guest:** He’s not interfering with you. He might not hurt you. But because you’re subject to his will, because you’re dependent upon him, you are unfree. And of course, that was the situation of English wives. I mean, subject to the common law system of coverture, your legal personality as a wife was literally subsumed, was covered by that of your husband.

**Guest:** Things that you supposedly owned, you didn’t actually own at all in the end. Yourself, your property was his. I should say that I’m making this argument, but also the brilliant philosopher Lena Haldanius has also done excellent work to talk about the way in which early modern feminism draws on this neo Roman theory of liberty in this very deep way. And Wollstonecraft is worried about freedom and unfreedom in this sense. Not just in so far as it’s a description of juridical status.

**Guest:** It’s not just the case that women are unfree as wives, and that’s a bad thing, which it clearly is. Interested, and other early modern feminists are very interested too, in the kind of the effects of unfreedom, the effects of living subject to the arbitrary power of another on a person. And the thought is, if you are dependent on men, you will do everything you can to please them. And so there’s a kind of abjection, a kind of hollowing out of the self that follows from this power relation. So you will construct yourself, as it were, through the male gaze and try to find the only power that is available to you within that structural circumstance, which is the power of the sexual object, beautiful because that’s the only kind of value that you have within that relation.

**Guest:** So it turns women into creatures who are interested in their appearance, who are interested in their beauty. It turns them inside out, not only with a kind of Bovarian sort of reduction, objection, loss of self, but also with a focus on superficiality, appearance. It turns women into cunning coquettes who find their power through flirtation and power games, competition with other women, rather than enabling them to construct virtuous, dutiful selves. So there’s a close relationship between freedom and morality. Freedom enables virtue and unfreedom degrades both parties, she says.

**Guest:** It degrades both the master and the slave, both the husband and the wife. The husband becomes arrogant and the woman becomes cunning and corrupt.

**Host 1:** So that’s a classic unequal relationship.

**Guest:** Absolutely. And I mean, you’ve nailed what I think is another absolutely crucial part of her philosophy, which is about the relationship between liberty and equality. So if it’s the case that hierarchy per se, that subjection to the will of another makes you unfree and has all of these terrible moral consequences, then the solution is a more equal society. And she does indeed say that at various points. And she’s thinking in terms of equality, sort of kind of egalitarian vision, not just in the context of gender relations, but also obviously in the context of politics.

**Guest:** She’s against absolute monarchy, but also in relation to the church or to the army. So running throughout the vindication of the rights of woman is actually an interest in, as it were, leveling out the hierarchies in order not just to promote freedom, but freedom as a route to a virtuous society. And she’s talking there also about class. She is interested in inequalities between rich and poor, and she thinks that they are pernicious as well.

**Host 1:** And this is quite amazing at the end of the eighteenth century that she seems to be prefiguring most of the themes of twentieth century feminism.

**Guest:** I mean, I have found her an extraordinarily powerful voice in the feminist tradition precisely insofar as she illuminates the psychological fallout of structural oppression and spells out how the personal is political. And this, of course, takes us right back to where we began thinking about the relationship between her life and her philosophy. One of the central tenets of feminist philosophy is that the personal is political. That what happens behind closed doors in the domestic sphere is itself shaped by relations of power. And also that what it feels like to crack up under pressure, to crack up in a marriage, to crack up in oppressive circumstances is actually not a kind of problem that’s personal to you, but is rather a function of structural inequality.

**Guest:** So to understand the personal as political to and to see that one can’t hope to give a full account of political philosophy without thinking about what happens in the family, without thinking about what happens in the private sphere, so called, is an inadequate political philosophy.

**Host 1:** Did she have anything to say about what’s natural for a woman?

**Guest:** Yes. Wollstonecraft is very interested in deconstructing sex and gender. And indeed, I don’t really think she thinks of a particular distinction between the two. And understanding the ways in which sex and gender are themselves, to a large extent, cultural constructs. So when she’s talking about the differences between the sexes, she wants to say that actually this is a function of a corrupt civilization that men and women have created so differently.

**Guest:** It’s a function of one set being educated and the other set not. One set being allowed to kind of frolic about in the open air and the other being locked up behind closed doors doing needlework and playing with dolls. And she’s very interesting actually about the kind of relationship between the body and the mind and the way in which women’s bodies are themselves kind of constricted. And that, of course, has effect on their strength, on their power, on their persons. And I think she’s interested in minimizing the distinction between the sexes.

**Guest:** She thinks, you know, that at core, men and women are rational creatures. They’re the creatures of God. I mean, God’s an important figure here. She believes in a providentially organized universe and that God has given reason to men and women and that it’s part of his plan and it’s a kind of blasphemy not to let that reason flourish in women. And that if that reason was allowed to flourish and their bodies were allowed to kind of move about just as men’s bodies move about, that actually you’d have creatures that were very hard to distinguish.

**Guest:** And that they were more united by their humanity than they were divided by any distinction of sex. You know, if you think about subsequent history of feminism and the question of the construction of gender, I think Walston Kraft is absolutely there saying that these are constructed entities and the project project is to deconstruct them and liberate women from the strictures of gender.

**Host 1:** One last question. If somebody was gonna read this book for the first time, how should they approach it?

**Guest:** Ah, well, the amazing thing about Wollstonecraft that we haven’t talked about is that she is such an extraordinary writer. You know, she’s a romantic writer. She’s living in that era. So the prose is gorgeous. And, you know, I give a warning, if you like, about the misogyny that I talked about earlier.

**Guest:** The people who come under the most criticism and the vindication of the rights of women are women. But not to see that as her criticizing women per se, but criticizing the society that has made them that way and wanting to liberate them from that oppressive structure.

**Host 1:** Hannah Dawson, thank you very much.

**Guest:** Thank you, Nigel. It’s been lovely.
